So I'm just trying to get a feel. Well, I mean, so did you raise a hypothetical or do you happen to believe that in our questioning whether you should? No way. I'm not a hypothetical. So I'm just trying to determine, I mean, common sense obviously plays into this, but if you're normally a private person, it's hard for you to reveal stuff anyway. I was just trying to get a guy. Well, okay, again, let's look at candidates. Let's look at Ron Paul, who Ron just set the single day fundraising record on the Internet, right? And Ron's been running since, I don't know when. He used to run as a libertarian and then he finally got elected to Congress as a Republican, but really he's a libertarian. And so, you know, Ron has a number of extreme positions. They prohibit him, of course, from getting the presidential nomination of any recognized party, and they prohibit him from, of course, winning the general election should he get that nomination. He has no such intent of doing either one of those two things. So you have to think about what your intent is, right? So Ron Paul is about getting a lot of support from a small microcosm of society who are rabid fans, who he has enormous influence over, right? And who will come and pay to see him speak after this is all over with, who will buy books, who will buy tapes, and they're rabid, right? Playboy was built on the Playboy philosophy. And there is profound philosophy, and Hefner enunciated it. And he kind of got annoyed at some point, and actually then started to write about it in a series of installments. If you don't have them, they're very interesting. So they ran, I think, in the 60s. And he, over a period of umpteen issues, laid out what the Playboy philosophy was. And his greatest influence over people had to do with the philosophy, not with the pictures. And it annoyed, and endless, I mean, so it irritated religious people. It irritated a large percentage of the female population. It irritated all the people that weren't going to give him money anyway, but had enormous resonance with the segment of the population to whom he was trying to appeal. And the more you try and avoid offending and repelling and annoying people and being criticized by them, the less influence you have over the ones you got. We had a conversation yesterday. Tucker was here for a consulting day. So we named a segment of his market yesterday. So like Hillary has this, the Hillary campaign, for example, is focused on what they call women with needs, which is their description for women of a certain demographic, a certain age. They tend to be single moms. They tend to be working in low wage, blue collar jobs. And so they're looking to somebody to provide daycare, education, healthcare. They have a lot of needs that aren't being met. And so they're looking for somebody to meet them, which in this case is government. And so they call that women with needs. But I mean, there's just more women who now are exhibiting what I would call male business characteristics and more guys who have been oprified. You know, and they aren't such good customers. Well, I mean, look, 10 years ago, I never had the conversations I have today with men about, you know, I can't come to the seminar because if I don't rock the cradle every night, my child will grow up to be, you know, I've got to share the childcare load and I've got to be home for about, you didn't have those, and of course, 20 years ago, you never had the conversations, it was unheard of. 10 years ago, you never had the conversation. You had a conversation all the time now. See, that used to be kind of a uniquely female obstacle to us extracting money from them. And now it's increasingly a male obstacle and actually less of a female obstacle. But the women agents come to a three-day deal and their husband stays home from work and takes care of the kid. See, that's like new, right? And so that's changed a little bit. But by and large, if I'm going to offend somebody, I'd rather, you know, offend them. And they're still terrible clients. I mean, when you get to the client stage, they're awful. Because they're too easily offended. And now there might be good clients for somebody else, by the way, right? Okay, so staying on philosophy for a moment. We're not going to do all three of the exercises, but we are going to do one. And you don't actually need to move to do this. You can really stay where you are. But what I'm looking for, that you may not have actually given a lot of thought to before, is a concise statement, not of your total philosophy about each subject, because there would be many concise statements, but a concise statement about some aspect of your philosophy, about each of the topics on 42, about money, about business, about success, about sales, about time, about religion, or spirituality, if you prefer, about politics and about health and fitness. So I'm looking for a statement of profound core belief that you have about each one of those topics. And at the same time, jot down one you think I have. So you guys all know me. Now we'll see how good a job I've done at conveying what I want known. So get a core belief of your own for each one of those things, and quickly get a core belief of mine. And then I want to talk about a few of the examples. So quick as you can, like 10 minutes, go through this process.
